content: Press enter or click to view image in full size 3 min read 1 hour ago -- Why the "obvious" payload fails on an innerHTML sink, and what actually pops the alert. Goal: Perform a DOM-based cross-site scripting attack on the blog’s search functionality that calls the alert function. Press enter or click to view image in full size Setup: PortSwigger lab , a blog site with a search box. The search term isn’t reflected server-side this time; it’s read straight out of location.search on the client and written into the page using innerHTML. That distinction matters, because innerHTML doesn’t behave the same way a raw HTML response does. Steps 1. Land on the blog home page and check out the search box before touching anything: Press enter or click to view image in full size 2. Pop open DevTools and read the source. A script block runs doSearchQuery() on page load, pulls the search parameter straight out of location.search, and assigns it directly to searchMessage.innerHTML with zero sanitization: function doSearchQuery(query) { document.getElementById('searchMessage').innerHTML = query; } var query = (new URLSearchParams(window.location.search)).get('search'); if(query) { doSearchQuery(query); }That confirms the sink (innerHTML) and the source (location.search) — an unencoded DOM XSS. 3. Reach for the "obvious" payload next: Press enter or click to view image in full size 5. Submit it and check the Elements panel the
